skill.name: casely
skill.description: Intelligent QA assistant that automates writing test cases from project documentation. Use when the user wants to generate test cases from requirements, runs /init, /parse, /style, /plan, /generate, /export, or works with PDF/DOCX/XLSX requirement documents and TestRail-ready Excel export.
skill.summary: Automated QA test case generation from requirement documents with style-consistent output
skill.author: JohnWayneeee

security_audit.summary: All 123 static analysis findings are false positives. The 'external_commands' patterns are markdown code blocks in documentation showing example bash commands, not actual shell execution. The 'blocker' and 'weak cryptography' findings are from imported libraries (docling, openpyxl) used for document parsing and Excel generation. The skill code itself contains no security vulnerabilities, malicious intent, or dangerous patterns. This is a legitimate QA automation tool.

content.user_title: Generate test cases from documentation automatically
content.value_statement: Casely eliminates 40% of QA engineering time by converting requirement documents into structured test cases. It learns your team's writing style from examples and exports TestRail-ready Excel files.
actual_capabilities: Convert PDF, DOCX, XLSX, and other document formats to Markdown using docling
Analyze existing test case examples to extract column structure and writing style
Generate atomic test cases that match your team's exact formatting conventions
Create comprehensive test plans with coverage estimation by module and test type
Export Markdown test cases to formatted Excel files with proper column mapping
Support multiple languages for test case generation based on example language
limitations: Requires Python dependencies (docling, openpyxl) to be installed via uv or pip
Only processes document formats supported by docling library
Test case quality depends on clarity and completeness of source requirement documents
Style guide generation requires existing example test cases from your team

use_cases.title: New feature test coverage
use_cases.description: QA engineers receive a 50-page requirements document for a new payments feature. Casely parses the document, analyzes existing test case examples, and generates 200+ atomic test cases matching the team's format.

use_cases.title: Test case migration to TMS
use_cases.description: A team has hundreds of test cases in various document formats. Casely converts them all to Markdown, applies consistent formatting, and exports to Excel for import into TestRail or other test management systems.

use_cases.title: Rapid test plan creation
use_cases.description: During sprint planning, a QA analyst needs to estimate testing effort. Casely scans requirements, identifies modules, and generates a comprehensive test plan with coverage tiers (smoke, critical, full) and case estimates.

prompt_templates.title: Initialize new project
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new testing project with requirement documents

prompt_templates.title: Parse requirement documents
prompt_templates.scenario: Converting uploaded PDF/DOCX/XLSX files to Markdown for analysis

prompt_templates.title: Create style guide from examples
prompt_templates.scenario: Analyzing existing test cases to extract formatting patterns

prompt_templates.title: Generate test cases for module
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating atomic test cases for a specific feature module

output_examples.output: Creates project structure, parses documents, generates style guide, creates test plan with 45 estimated cases, generates functional test cases for checkout flow, and exports to Excel files in exports/ folder.

output_examples.input: Generate negative test cases for the payment processing module based on the requirements and style guide.
output_examples.output: Creates individual Markdown files for each negative test scenario (invalid card numbers, expired cards, declined transactions, etc.) matching the exact column structure from analyzed examples.

best_practices: Always run /parse after uploading new requirement documents to ensure latest content is processed
Review and edit the generated test_style_guide.md before running /generate to ensure accurate formatting
Use /plan first to understand scope and estimate test coverage before generating full test suites
Run /export immediately after /generate to convert Markdown files to Excel for TMS import
anti_patterns: Do not skip the /style step as it ensures generated test cases match your team's exact format
Do not mix test types in a single generation command - use separate /generate calls for each type
Do not edit generated test case Markdown files directly - regenerate with updated style guide instead
Do not run /export before completing all /generate operations for consistency

faq.question: What document formats does Casely support?
faq.answer: Casely supports PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, HTML, HTM, MD, TXT, PNG, JPG, JPEG, TIFF, and TIF files through the docling library.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything to use Casely?
faq.answer: Yes, Casely requires Python dependencies docling and openpyxl. The skill uses the uv package manager for fast installation.

faq.question: Can Casely generate test cases in languages other than English?
faq.answer: Yes, Casely detects the language of your example test cases and generates new test cases in the same language.

faq.question: What is the difference between /parse and /style?
faq.answer: /parse converts document files to Markdown format. /style analyzes those parsed examples to extract your team's test case structure and writing patterns.

faq.question: How do I import the exported Excel files into my test management system?
faq.answer: The exported Excel files have columns matching your style guide. Most TMS platforms like TestRail have Excel import wizards that map columns automatically.

faq.question: Can I modify the generated test cases?
faq.answer: Yes, you can edit individual Markdown test case files or update the style guide and regenerate. However, manual edits to Markdown files will be lost if you regenerate.
